chosen_title: ITR filing 2026: Have foreign assets or income? Not reporting them may cost you ₹10 lakh
caption:
That old ESOP. That forgotten US bank account. That foreign FD you never closed.
If it's not in your ITR this July 31 — it could cost you ₹10 lakh.

India's Black Money Act makes it mandatory for every resident taxpayer to disclose foreign assets and income in Schedule FA of their ITR — every single year, even if the asset earned nothing. Foreign bank accounts, ESOPs, RSUs, shares in a foreign company, overseas property, a beneficial interest in a foreign trust — all of it must be declared.

The penalty is a flat ₹10 lakh per year of non-disclosure. No warnings, no sliding scale. And the assessing officer can go back multiple years.

For illustration: Rajan worked at a US MNC, held ESOPs, and had a US bank account. He returned to India in 2023 but skipped Schedule FA in FY24 and FY25. Potential exposure: ₹10L × 2 years = ₹20 lakh — on assets that may have earned almost nothing. (Illustration only; actual liability depends on individual facts.)

What to do: Check your AIS on the income tax portal, dig out ESOP and broker statements, and disclose every foreign asset in Schedule FA before July 31, 2026.

Not sure what qualifies? Drop us a WhatsApp — we'll sort it out quickly.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

Source: Black Money (Undisclosed Foreign Income and Assets) and Imposition of Tax Act, 2015; Income Tax Act, 1961.

slides.headline: The ₹10 lakh fine hiding in your ITR

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
slides.headline: Foreign asset + silence = ₹10 lakh penalty
slides.body: India's Black Money Act requires every resident taxpayer to disclose foreign assets and income in Schedule FA of their ITR — every year, even if the asset earned zero. Miss the disclosure and the penalty is a flat ₹10 lakh per year of non-filing. No warnings. No proportionality. The clock is ticking: deadline is July 31, 2026.

slides.label: ITR
slides.title: What counts as a 'foreign asset'?
points: Foreign bank accounts — including NRE/NRO accounts you forgot to close after returning to India.
ESOPs, RSUs, or shares in a foreign company received from your employer.
Foreign immovable property — land, apartment, or any ownership interest abroad.
Beneficial interest in a foreign trust, partnership, or any overseas entity.
Any foreign-sourced income — overseas salary, dividends, rent, or capital gains.

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
slides.body: Flat penalty per year of non-disclosure under the Black Money Act — Section 42. The assessing officer can go back multiple assessment years. Two missed ITRs can stack up to ₹20 lakh in fines, even if the underlying asset earned very little.

slides.label: ITR
slides.headline: Illustration: Rajan's ₹20 lakh oversight
slides.body: Rajan worked at a US MNC, received ESOPs, and held a US bank account. He returned to India in 2023 — but skipped Schedule FA in both FY24 and FY25 ITRs, assuming the amounts were too small to matter. Potential exposure: ₹10 lakh × 2 years = ₹20 lakh. On assets that barely earned anything. This is an illustration only; actual outcomes depend on individual facts and officer discretion.

slides.label: ITR
slides.title: 4 things to do before July 31, 2026
points: Open your Annual Information Statement (AIS) on the income tax portal and check for any foreign credits or entries.
Locate all ESOP/RSU grant letters, foreign broker statements, and overseas bank account details — even dormant ones.
Disclose every foreign account, asset, and income in Schedule FA of your ITR, even if the balance or income was zero.
Missed a previous year? Talk to a CA about a revised or updated return — acting early is always better than waiting for a notice.
